Title: Khaled: Defence self-reliance key to national security
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Countries should not depend on others for their defence and must instead build their own military capabilities, Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin said.
Khaled was responding to a call by United States Secretary of Defence Pete Hegseth for Asian allies and partners to increase defence spending to 3.5 per cent of gross domestic product (GDP), saying the policy is a fair request and aligned with Malaysia's longstanding approach to defence self-reliance.
"We take note of the US policy and views, especially the new policy that every partner or friendly country needs to be capable of defending its own country and not be dependent on the US.
"We accept that. I think that is a very fair request. It is a good policy and a clear policy of the US," he said.
Earlier, Hegseth told delegates at the International Institute for Strategic Studies (IISS) Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore that the US expected its Asian allies and partners to increase defence spending, while pledging US$1.5 trillion in investments for its own military.
Hegseth also said that allies wanted stability rather than escalation and that the US would continue to provide strength, steady resolve and confident leadership.
Khaled added that Malaysia had long practised self-reliance in defence and did not depend on any single country to safeguard its national security.
"Like Malaysia, we take care of our own defence and, because we are neutral, we receive many programmes from many countries to build our capacity.
"We do not really depend on other countries to help us in our defence. We must make sure that we are capable ourselves," he said.
In Budget 2026, the Defence Ministry was allocated RM21.7 billion, marking a RM600 million increase from 2025 and accounting for about one per cent of the country's GDP.
Earlier this year, the government also began implementing the National Defence Industry Policy, a strategic move aimed at strengthening the country's defence industry ecosystem, enhancing local capabilities and reducing long-term reliance on foreign defence procurement through greater participation by domestic companies in defence supply chains and technology development.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Four more men trapped in semi-submerged Laos cave freed: rescuers
Article:
VIENTIANE: Four more men trapped in a semi-submerged Laos cave for 10 days have been freed, rescuers said, after one was successfully brought to the surface a day earlier, but two remained missing.
"4 victim is out," Lee Kian Lie, a Malaysian rescue diver, told AFP via Messenger.
"Water level is lowered by pumping, so they able to come out," he said.
The Thailand Rescue Diver Facebook page said in a post that "rescue officials were able to bring out four more people trapped" at about 3:10 pm (0810 GMT) on Saturday.
"A total of five people have now been rescued, while two remain missing," it said.
Seven men had been trapped in the cavern in a remote mountainous area of central Xaysomboun province since May 20, when flash floods stranded them as they searched for gold, state media reports said.
Five of the men were located alive on Wednesday, huddling in a narrow shaft around 300 metres (984 feet) from the cave mouth.
Rescuers guided a bedraggled and muddy man over unsteady ground to safety on Friday.
The seven were trapped after heavy rains triggered flash flooding that blocked the exit of the cavern. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Engineer found dead in locked car died of carbon monoxide poisoning
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: A 37-year-old engineer who was found dead inside a parked car along Jalan Sungai Dua yesterday died of carbon monoxide poisoning.
Timur Laut acting district police chief Superintendent Lee Swee Sake said the cause of death was determined following a post-mortem at the Penang Hospital forensic department today.
"The post-mortem found that the victim died due to carbon monoxide poisoning, and there were no elements of foul play. The case has been classified as sudden death, and a sudden death report has been opened for further investigation.
"The victim is also believed to have died about five days before his body was discovered yesterday," he said when contacted.
It is understood that the victim had fallen asleep in the vehicle without switching off the engine.
Yesterday, the body of the victim, who was from Ayer Itam, was found fully dressed in the
driver's seat
of his wife's car, which was locked from the inside, at 11.47am.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar: Ministry savings redirected to programmes that benefit the people
Article:
PENAMPANG: The Madani Government has imposed strict cost-cutting measures across ministries, with all savings channelled into programmes that directly benefit the people.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said cuts would focus on non-essential spending, while key sectors such as education, healthcare and basic infrastructure, including in Sabah, would remain unaffected.
Anwar said the cost-cutting measures include limiting ministers' overseas trips, except for mandatory meetings under international platforms such as Asean, Brics and Apec.
"All study visits and similar trips have been cancelled at the federal level. Even for official visits, if a delegation previously consisted of six people, it is now reduced to two. If the accommodation was a Royal Suite, it is now downgraded to a Junior Suite," he said.
Anwar said this when officiating the Sabah-level Kaamatan Festival 2026 celebration at Hongkod Koisaan, the headquarters of the Kadazandusun Cultural Association (KDCA), here today.
Also present were Chief Minister Datuk Seri Hajiji Noor, Deputy Chief Minister I Datuk Seri Dr Joachim Gunsalam, who is also chairman of the Sabah Kaamatan Festival 2026 organising committee, and KDCA president Tan Sri Joseph Pairin Kitingan.
Apart from overseas travel, Anwar said government training programmes are now being held at government-owned facilities instead of luxury hotels.
"If we do not exercise wisdom and prudence in spending, the country cannot be saved. The money we save is channelled back to the people," he said.
The prime minister also stressed that efforts to strengthen governance and combat corruption were not politically motivated, but aimed at restoring the country's financial position.
He said savings generated through cost-cutting measures and the recovery of funds had enabled the government to channel RM15.5 billion back to the people through various initiatives, including the Sumbangan Tunai Rahmah (STR) and Sumbangan Asas Rahmah (Sara) programmes.
The funds have also been used for the repair of tens of thousands of school toilets at an additional cost of RM1 billion, as well as the immediate distribution of RM100 book vouchers to all school and higher education students nationwide, Anwar said.
Meanwhile, in addressing an increasingly uncertain global geopolitical landscape, Anwar said Malaysia's foreign policy is based on the principle of 'centrality', which allows the country to remain independent while maintaining a principled position on global issues, rather than merely being neutral.
"We do not use neutrality because there are times when injustice, violence and killings occur elsewhere, and we do not think it is right to remain silent. Centrality means being independent," he said, referring to Malaysia's vocal stance against oppression.
He added that Malaysia's principled and investor-friendly foreign policy had also contributed to stronger trade and investment ties with both the United States and China.
"Their visits here and our visits to their countries are driven by the same spirit - to benefit our people and our nation," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PKR election director reshuffle a strategic move, says AMK
Article:
PADANG RENGAS: The change in PKR's election director post is part of a strategic restructuring exercise aimed at strengthening the party machinery ahead of the next election.
Angkatan Muda Keadilan (AMK) chief Muhammad Kamil Abdul Munim said changes in portfolios, whether within a political party or the government, are a normal practice undertaken based on current needs.
He said the restructuring of responsibilities within the party should not be construed as reflecting any weakness on the part of its leaders, including Nurul Izzah Anwar, who now serves as PKR deputy president.
"There is no question of her being unqualified or incapable because she previously served as election director, including during the 14th General Election, when the party successfully formed the government.
"This is merely a strategic restructuring to ensure election-related operations can be carried out more effectively and efficiently," he told reporters at the Madani Youth Carnival in Padang Rengas today.
On the
appointment of PKR
vice-president Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari as the party's election director alongside Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail, Muhammad Kamil said both leaders possess extensive experience in managing election operations and organisation.
"Previously, Amirudin served as election operations director, giving him the knowledge and experience required. I am confident he will be able to carry out this responsibility effectively," he said.
Meanwhile, commenting on Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim's remarks on the possibility of cost-saving measures in Budget 2027 amid global economic uncertainty, he described the move as a responsible approach.
"Any responsible leader would review the country's economic position and align expenditure with current needs. Although such measures may be viewed as unpopular, they are important to ensure the long-term sustainability of the country's economy," he said.
He also saida that any restructuring of government expenditure would not affect critical sectors such as healthcare and education.
"Related programmes will continue to be implemented, but the government will review its spending methods and approaches to identify areas where savings can be made," he said. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Don't politicise federal funds, Umno Youth tells PH [WATCH]
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Umno Youth has hit back at Pakatan Harapan (PH) leaders over claims that Johor's recent development was largely driven by federal funding.
Its secretary-general Hafiz Ariffin said the billions of ringgit frequently cited by PH included projects that have yet to materialise on the ground.
"The RM14.6 billion allocation repeatedly highlighted by PH leaders comprised a mix of announced projects, operational expenditure and federal administrative spending, and should not be portrayed as direct financial assistance to the Johor state government," he said in a Facebook post.
Hafiz said major projects such as Sultanah Aminah Hospital 2 (HSA2) and the proposed Elevated Autonomous Rapid Transit (E-ART) system remain in the pipeline and have yet to be fully implemented.
"Announcing a project is not the same as having the funds channelled and spent on the ground.
"Nobody is denying the federal government's contributions, but don't use federal development and expenditure in Johor as a political tool to create the perception that Johor is overclaiming and ungrateful," he said in a video accompanying the post.
The latest exchange marks an escalation in tensions between Barisan Nasional (BN) and PH following Johor Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi's declaration that BN would contest all 56 state seats in the next state election.
The announcement has exposed growing strains within the unity government coalition, with leaders from both camps publicly sparring over political cooperation and credit for Johor's economic achievements.
Hafiz questioned why PH leaders had chosen to raise the issue of federal allocations now, suggesting it was linked to BN's decision to contest the election on its own.
"Why bring up these calculations now? Are you trying to use political cooperation as a measure of development?
"If that is the case, I think the people of Johor will make their own calculations when the time comes," he said.
His remarks were directed at
DAP secretary-general and Transport Minister Anthony Loke,
who told the recent PH Convention in Johor that many of the state's recent successes stemmed from federal initiatives and support.
Hafiz, who is also BN Youth secretary-general, said the RM14.6 billion figure often cited by PH included routine federal expenditure such as civil service salaries, school operations and healthcare spending.
"These are federal government expenses in Johor. They should not be portrayed as though they are direct cash transfers to the state government," he said.
He also argued that Johor was among the country's largest contributors to federal revenue, citing Inland Revenue Board figures showing that the state generated nearly RM20 billion of Malaysia's
RM203.9 billion direct tax collection
last year.
The exchange comes amid growing speculation that Johor could be heading for an early state election, with both BN and PH increasingly sharpening their messaging as political temperatures rise in the state.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mustapha: RM1.5bil interim payment proves federal govt's commitment to Sabah
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: The increase in Sabah's interim special grant payment from RM600 million to RM1.5 billion reflects the federal government's continued commitment to addressing the state's rights claims in a gradual, structured and responsible manner.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Sabah and Sarawak Affairs) Datuk Mustapha Sakmud said the increase underscored the Madani government's commitment under Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim to ensure Sabah's rights continue to receive due attention.
He said the announcement demonstrated that the government was not only listening to the voices of Sabahans but was also taking concrete steps to strengthen the state's position within the federation.
"This announcement is yet another clear demonstration of the commitment, courage and determination of the Madani government under the Prime Minister's leadership in addressing Sabah's rights claims in a gradual, structured and fully responsible manner.
"This consistent increase proves that the voices and demands of the people of Sabah are not only being heard but are also being given serious attention by the Federal Government," he said in a statement today.
Mustapha said that before the change of federal government in 2022, Sabah had received only about RM26.7 million annually for decades, despite its 40 per cent revenue entitlement being enshrined in the Federal Constitution.
He said special payments to Sabah rose significantly under Anwar's administration, increasing to RM125.6 million in 2022, RM300 million in 2023 and 2024, before being raised to RM600 million for 2025 and 2026.
He said this year's increase to RM1.5 billion marked a significant step towards strengthening Sabah's financial position while a broader resolution to the state's 40 per cent revenue claim is being finalised.
"We must be fair in assessing this development. Sabah's 40 per cent revenue claim has persisted for decades and cannot be resolved within a short period without strong political commitment and close cooperation between the state and Federal governments.
"As such, the RM1.5 billion announcement is a major achievement that should be appreciated by all Sabahans," he said.
Mustapha said efforts to pursue Sabah's rights would continue until a more comprehensive and final resolution is achieved in line with the spirit of the Malaysia Agreement 1963 (MA63) and Articles 112C and 112D of the Federal Constitution.
He said a mature, prudent and forward-looking approach must be maintained to ensure Sabah's rights are realised without affecting stability and good relations within the Federation of Malaysia.
"I am confident that under the prime minister's leadership and the spirit of Malaysia Madani, the agenda to empower Sabah and Sarawak will remain a priority of the federal government," he said.
Earlier today, Anwar said the interim special grant for Sabah has been increased to RM1.5 billion this year from RM600 million previously.
www.nst.com.my/news/regional/2026/05/1451448/updated-anwar-sabah-interim-special-grant-increased-rm15-billion
He said the increase is a temporary measure pending a final mechanism for Sabah's 40 per cent revenue entitlement under the Malaysia Agreement 1963 (MA63).
He said this when officiating the 2026 Sabah-level Kaamatan Festival at Hongkod Koisaan, Kadazandusun Cultural Association, in Penampang.
Ends
Keywords: news, NST, Malaysia, Sabah, Mustapha Sakmud, Anwar Ibrahim, MA63, Sabah rights, special grant, interim payment, Federal Constitution, Sabah revenue entitlement, Kaamatan Festival, Penampang, Federal Government
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Search teams retrieve camera memory chips to track missing hiker
Article:
TAPAH: Search teams have been deployed to retrieve surveillance camera memory chips along the Gunung Batu Putih hiking trail to check if a woman missing for a week passed through the area.
Perak Fire and Rescue Department assistant operations director Sabarodzi Nor Ahmad said the task was being jointly undertaken by a Department of Wildlife and National Parks (Perhilitan) protection team and 16 rescue personnel.
He said they were flown to the search area this morning aboard an AW139 helicopter and lowered via winching at Kem Kantoi.
"The protection team, together with the rescue team, will retrieve surveillance camera chips around the trail area to monitor whether the victim can be detected through the surveillance camera route.
"The total number of rescue personnel deployed and stationed at the search location this morning is 50," he said in a statement today.
Meanwhile, the Perak Mountain Guides Association, in a Facebook post, said the operation to locate Jaslinda Saludin, 49, had also received support from out-of-state mountain guides who joined the search mission.
"We seek everyone's prayers that Jaslinda will be found as soon as possible. This search is highly challenging as its radius covers elevations of around 3,000 to 7,000 feet and is very extensive. So far, all parties are doing their best," the post said.
Jaslinda began the Trans Spencer Chapman hike at 2am on May 23 with 13 other hikers and two Forestry Mountain Guides (MGPs) via the Pos Gedung-Gunung Bah Gading-Gunung Batu Putih-Kuala Woh route.
Jaslinda and another hiker, Mohd Hanafi Neikmad, 41, reportedly experienced health issues and stopped, but
Jaslinda later continued towards the summit and was last seen by a mountain guide at 7.30am on May 24
.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ewon hails RM1.5bil interim payment for Sabah
Article:
PENAMPANG: United Progressive Kinabalu Organisation (Upko) president Datuk Ewon Benedick has welcomed Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim's commitment to implement Sabah's 40 per cent revenue entitlement as provided under the Federal Constitution.
The commitment includes increasing the interim payment to Sabah from RM600 million to RM1.5 billion.
Ewon, who is also Sabah Deputy Chief Minister III and Entrepreneur Development and Cooperatives Minister, said the announcement reflected the federal government's commitment to progressively fulfil Sabah's claims relating to its revenue rights.
He said this was being done while negotiations between the federal government and the Sabah government continued towards a final settlement.
"On behalf of Upko, I welcome the prime minister's commitment to implement Sabah's 40 per cent entitlement and the decision to increase the interim payment to RM1.5 billion," he said.
He was speaking after attending the 2026 Sabah-level Kaamatan Festival celebration at Hongkod Koisaan here, which was officiated by the prime minister today.
Ewon said Upko would continue to support efforts aimed at restoring Sabah's rights in accordance with the spirit of the Malaysia Agreement 1963 (MA63) and the Federal Constitution.
He described the Kaamatan Festival as an important platform for strengthening unity among Sabah's multi-ethnic communities.
"Kaamatan is not merely a cultural celebration but also a symbol of gratitude, unity and the identity of the people of Sabah, which must continue to be preserved and passed on to future generations," he said.
Ewon was commenting on the prime minister's statement regarding the federal government's commitment to implementing Sabah's 40 per cent revenue entitlement as provided under the Federal Constitution and MA63.
In his speech, Anwar said the ongoing negotiations are aimed at finalising the implementation mechanism.
This includes the legal and financial aspects governing relations between the Federal Government and the Sabah government.
He added that while the negotiations are being finalised, the federal government would increase the interim payment to Sabah from RM600 million to RM1.5 billion.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sabah's growth exceeds projections, KKIA upgrade to be expedited – Anwar
Article:
PENAMPANG: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said rapid growth in Sabah's development and tourism sector has surpassed earlier projections, prompting the federal government to expedite infrastructure upgrading works, particularly at Kota Kinabalu International Airport (KKIA).
He said the significant increase in tourist arrivals to the state has created an urgent need to improve and expand existing facilities.
"Under normal projections, there would be no need to expand infrastructure at such a pace.
"However, Sabah's growth has surpassed normal projections and recorded development that is truly commendable," he said when launching the state-level Kaamatan Festival 2026 at Hongkod Koisaan, Kadazandusun Cultural Association (KDCA) here today.
Also present were Sabah Chief Minister Datuk Seri Hajiji Noor, Deputy Chief Minister I Datuk Seri Dr Joachim Gunsalam, and KDCA president Tan Sri Joseph Pairin Kitingan.
In his speech, Anwar praised Hajiji's leadership, describing his administration as moderate and focused on development, stability, and well-being, which has contributed to one of the strongest tourism growth rates in the country.
To ensure that no citizen or region is left behind, he said poverty eradication programmes and assistance initiatives such as Sumbangan Tunai Rahmah (STR) and Sumbangan Asas Rahmah (Sara) are being implemented nationwide.
"The difference is that we must accelerate and intensify our efforts in more underdeveloped areas. There are remote districts in Pahang, as well as rural and interior areas in Sabah and Sarawak. Therefore, our focus must be directed towards these areas," he said.
On the Kaamatan Festival, Anwar said he was proud to have made the cultural celebration an annual event in his official calendar.
He said this reflected the Madani government's commitment not only to promoting racial, religious, and cultural diversity, but also to translating those principles into tangible action.
He added that official events organised under his administration have never sidelined the cultural participation of Sabah communities, while policies implemented by the Tourism, Arts and Culture Ministry consistently ensure the full inclusion of the country's diverse cultural traditions.
"To become a state leader, let alone a national leader, one must project a character that reflects not only concern for one's own race, religion or community, but also the capacity to understand and care for all communities in our country.
"The same applies to state leaders and leaders of ethnic communities. They must embrace diversity. The moment we focus solely on the interests of a particular group, it runs contrary to the spirit of unity and undermines our culture," he said. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: KL book fair reflects reader demand, not exclusion, says organiser
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Organisers of the Kuala Lumpur International Book Fair (KLIBF) 2026 have defended the event against criticism over the diversity of books, publishers, and authors featured at the annual fair, saying participation remains open to all and is ultimately driven by market demand.
Perbadanan Kota Buku chief executive officer Adibah Omar said publishers of all genres are welcome to participate in the event, which operates as a commercial platform shaped largely by reader demand and industry participation.
Her remarks came amid discussions on Threads tagged "KLIBF", where users debated whether the fair's offerings reflected its status as an international book fair.
One user, naimtamdjis, questioned the event's international credentials, arguing that major international publishers and globally recognised authors were largely absent while Malay-language titles appeared to dominate the fair.
Another user, zzerosemoa, said they found it unusual that the event was branded as an international book fair despite not seeing publishers such as Scholastic and Puffin listed among exhibitors.
Some users also raised concerns over the range of genres available, suggesting that scholarly and non-fiction works appeared less prominent than more commercially popular categories.
Others, however, defended the fair.
User fatihah__hasmi noted that many international titles are available through Malaysian distributors and argued that visitors could still find foreign-language books and internationally published works at the fair.
Another user, faqir_khairul, said publishers ultimately cater to demand, arguing that not every genre attracts sufficient readership to justify exhibition space.
Responding to the discussion, Adibah said publishers from all segments of the industry, including independent publishers, remain welcome to participate.
"Independent publishers have always been welcome to participate.
"In fact, we previously allocated booths specifically for independent publishers.
"However, we also want them to assimilate with larger commercial publishers to help strengthen and empower them," she said today.
Critics questioned the fair's international credentials. Adibah responded by noting that nine international exhibitors from countries including Saudi Arabia, China, Indonesia, Iran and the United Kingdom are participating this year.
She also said booth registration has been fully automated in recent years and operates on a first-come, first-served basis, adding that Malaysia is the first country in Southeast Asia to implement such a system for a book fair.
"Demand has consistently exceeded supply.
"We only offered 1,200 booths, but received 1,400 applications," she said.
She also pointed to changing reading habits among Malaysians, particularly growing interest in non-fiction titles.
Adibah said philosophy books, once considered difficult to market, are now finding a growing audience among readers.
"Twenty years ago, these books would not have sold.
"Back then, people came to book fairs to buy academic workbooks," she said.
The shift, she added, reflects evolving reading preferences and a broader appetite for different categories of books.
KLIBF 2026 entered its second day today after being
opened by Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim
yesterday at the World Trade Centre Kuala Lumpur.
The event runs until June 7.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Search for missing hiker intensified with 16 more rescuers
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The search and rescue (SAR) operation for missing female hiker Jaslinda Saludin, has been intensified with the deployment of 16 additional rescuers.
The Perak Fire and Rescue Department said the additional rescuers comprise six members of the department's Special Tactical Operation and Rescue Team (Storm), two Department of Wildlife and National Parks (Perhilitan) protection team members, five Forestry Department personnel, and three Forestry Mountain Guides (MGPs).
It said all personnel were flown to the operation site aboard an AgustaWestland AW139 helicopter this morning.
"The team will be stationed at the summit of Gunung Batu Putih and the latest winching point at Kem Kantoi to reinforce search operations in high-risk and hard-to-access areas.
"In addition to the ground operations, the Perhilitan protection team and rescue personnel will retrieve surveillance camera chips located along several jungle trails for review and analysis.
"This measure aims to identify any clues that could help track the victim's movements during the period she has been missing," it said in a statement today.
According to the statement, 34 rescue personnel who have spent the night in the search zone since yesterday will continue their search in designated sectors.
"With the additional personnel sent in today, the total number of personnel involved and present at the operation site has increased to 50.
"The integrated SAR operation continues with the cooperation of various agencies to ensure efforts to locate the victim can be carried out more effectively and comprehensively," it said.
Meanwhile, the Perak Mountain Guides Association, in a Facebook post, said the search is highly challenging as the search radius is very extensive, covering elevations between 3,000 and 7,000 feet.
It said the search remains ongoing with the cooperation of various agencies, including the Fire and Rescue Department, Perhilitan, and the police.
"Mountain guides from Pahang, Johor, and Selangor have also arrived to assist in the search.
"We seek everyone's prayers that Jaslinda will be found as soon as possible," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Loke: Govt reviewing laws, surveillance to tackle railway cable theft
Article:
PADANG BESAR: The government is reviewing measures to tighten laws and strengthen the use of surveillance technology to address the rampant theft of railway cables.
Transport Minister Anthony Loke said the matter has been discussed with the Home Ministry to enforce stricter legal action against such offences, which are viewed as a national security issue.
He said cable theft cannot be treated as an ordinary theft because it directly impacts the smooth operation of train services and public safety.
"The Transport Ministry, together with Keretapi Tanah Melayu Bhd (KTMB) and the Railway Assets Corporation (RAC), is looking into using more effective surveillance technology to detect and prevent this activity from recurring.
"The first step we are looking at is from a technological standpoint, specifically how we can install better and more effective monitoring systems along the tracks," he said.
He said this after attending the official opening of the Tuanku Syed Sirajuddin Free Trade Zone, or the Perlis Inland Port (PIP) here today.
Loke said among the main challenges in addressing the issue are high copper prices and the country's extensive railway network, which spans over 1,000km.
"It is indeed difficult to monitor every kilometre of the track, especially in remote areas that are easily accessible to irresponsible parties," he said.
He said the ministry is also studying approaches and solutions used in other nations, given that the same issue occurs in many countries with rail systems.
"This is not just a problem in Malaysia, but happens worldwide. Some countries already have better methods to handle it," he said.
Meanwhile, he said repair works are actively underway following the recent incident where an LRT train derailed near the Chan Sow Lin station.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Suspected 'tekong darat', nine undocumented migrants held after chase ends in crash
Article:
TUMPAT: A Malaysian man and nine undocumented migrants were detained after the Perodua Myvi they were travelling in crashed into a villager's car at Kampung Tok Malik here this morning.
Southeast Brigade General Operations Force (GOF) commander, Senior Assistant Commissioner Ahmad Radzi Hussain, said the 21-year-old Malaysian is believed to be a tekong darat, a term used locally for individuals who transport or harbour undocumented migrants.
He said the arrests were made during a patrol under Op Taring Wawasan in Wakaf Bharu.
"GOF personnel detected a suspicious Perodua Myvi and attempted to stop the vehicle for inspection.
"However, the driver refused to comply and sped away. GOF personnel then pursued the vehicle.
"During the high-speed chase, the suspect's vehicle crashed into a villager's car.
"GOF personnel subsequently detained the driver and nine passengers," he said.
Ahmad Radzi said the nine passengers, aged between 21 and 55, were all Bangladeshi citizens and were found without valid travel documents.
"Our investigations revealed that the migrants had allegedly entered the country through Pulau Besar before travelling to Kota Baru.
"They planned to continue their journey from the Lembah Sireh bus station in search of employment in Malaysia after completing their work contracts in Cambodia," he said.
He said the suspect is believed to have been paid RM100 per migrant to transport them from Tumpat to Kota Baru.
Ahmad Radzi said the case is being investigated under Section 26A of the Anti-Trafficking in Persons and Anti-Smuggling of Migrants Act 2007 (Atipsom) and Section 6(1)(c) of the Immigration Act 1959/63.
"The driver and all nine migrants have been handed over to the Tumpat district police headquarters for further action," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: New Perlis inland port to strengthen Malaysia's role as regional logistics hub
Article:
PADANG BESAR: The government is confident that the development of the Perlis Inland Port (PIP), known as the Tuanku Syed Sirajuddin Free Trade Zone, will contribute significantly to the Malaysia-Thailand bilateral trade target of US$30 billion (RM119 billion) by 2027.
Transport Minister Anthony Loke said the strategic development will strengthen Malaysia's position as a regional logistics hub, particularly in the northern border area.
He said the PIP development would strengthen cross-border trade relations and expand the economic network into the Mekong region, China, and Central Asia.
"The PIP is a national strategic project under the Twelfth Malaysia Plan (12MP) implemented through cooperation between the federal government, the Perlis state government, and the private sector.
"Its implementation will not only improve the country's logistics efficiency but also strengthen Malaysia's competitiveness in an increasingly challenging global supply chain," he said at the official opening of the PIP by the Raja of Perlis, Tuanku Syed Sirajuddin Jamalullail.
Loke said the new facility was developed to replace the Padang Besar Cargo Terminal, which had reached its operational capacity limit of 150,000 TEUs annually.
He said the PIP, built on a 202-hectare site, offers an initial capacity of 600,000 TEUs per year, with the potential to increase to two million TEUs when fully completed.
He added that the PIP serves as an example of a multimodal transport system that integrates rail, road, and logistics within a single integrated ecosystem.
It will feature a direct connection to the national double-tracking rail network and the Thailand railway network, opening up broader access to Asean markets, China, Central Asia, and Europe via the Trans-Asia Railway route.
He said the development is also expected to stimulate the local economy through new job opportunities, increased business activities, and the influx of high-quality investments into Perlis.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fadhlina encourages Malaysians to embrace reading culture
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek is encouraging Malaysians to invest in books and cultivate a reading habit, stressing the importance of reading in building a knowledgeable society.
Speaking during an interview at the Kuala Lumpur International Book Fair (KLIBF) 2026 today, Fadhlina said Malaysia must ensure equitable access to books if it aspires to become a World Book Capital.
She added that the government would continue providing book vouchers to improve access to reading materials.
In conjunction with the event, Fadhlina also launched Kelab Perempuan Membaca (KPM).
"The club was established not only to encourage reading among the community but also to serve as a platform for knowledge-sharing," she said.
To foster a reading culture, Fadhlina said parents must play a key role by instilling reading discipline at home.
Also present at the event was Perbadanan Kota Buku chief executive officer Adibah Omar, who moderated the interview session with Fadhlina during the launch of Kelab Perempuan Membaca at KLIBF 2026.
National Laureate Datuk Dr Zurinah Hassan was also present.
Yesterday, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim announced book vouchers for secondary school students under the Education Ministry and students at public higher education institutions.
The event entered its second day today after being officially opened by Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim at the World Trade Centre Kuala Lumpur (WTCKL) on May 29.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US-Iran talks not a definitive fix for global energy market uncertainty, says Akmal
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Recent talks between the United States and Iran should not be viewed as a definitive solution to ongoing uncertainties in the global energy market, Economy Minister Datuk Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir said.
He said developments in the Middle East continue to warrant close attention as the geopolitical situation remains fluid and could still affect global oil supplies.
Akmal noted that Brent crude prices had fallen and remained below US$90 a barrel last week, while spot oil prices stayed under US$100 a barrel.
"This indicates that developments over the past week have had a positive effect on oil prices.
"However, it is premature to conclude that the issue has been resolved, as we are still awaiting further developments in the Middle East.
"While oil prices are expected to ease slightly further this week, the situation remains volatile," he told reporters after attending the Johor Baru parliamentary constituency's Perdana Aidiladha korban programme at Masjid Jamek Bandar Baru Uda today.
Akmal said the key concern now is ensuring the continued flow of physical crude oil supplies into the global market, particularly as the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world's most important energy shipping routes, has yet to be fully reopened.
Any disruption along the route, he said, could affect global energy supply chains and destabilise oil prices.
"The government will continue coordinating the necessary intervention measures to mitigate the impact of market uncertainty until the situation stabilises," he said.
Media reports previously stated that negotiators from the United States and Iran had reached a preliminary agreement to extend a 60-day ceasefire and establish a framework for further nuclear negotiations.
The memorandum of understanding is still subject to final approval by US President Donald Trump, although negotiators from both sides have reportedly finalised most of the agreement's terms.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'True heroine': Senator praises USM graduate for viral act of cross-cultural kindness
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: A young woman has been praised for quietly helping a needy man buy food and drinks at a cafe, in an act described as reflecting the compassion and unity that continue to exist in Malaysian society.
Senator Dr R. A. Lingeshwaran shared the incident on social media, saying he witnessed the encounter at a Coffee Bean outlet here this morning.
He said an underprivileged man had been approaching patrons and seeking assistance.
"Many people noticed him, but what happened next made me smile and feel proud to be Malaysian," he said in a Facebook post.
Lingeshwaran said a young woman, identified as Siti Zulaiqah, a graduate of Universiti Sains Malaysia, stepped forward to help.
Without hesitation, she accompanied the man to the counter and invited him to choose the food and drinks he wanted before paying for the items, he said.
"There were no cameras, no publicity and no intention of seeking praise. It was simply a small act born from a big heart," he added.
Describing Siti as an example of kindness in action, Lingeshwaran said meaningful acts did not require wealth, power or status.
"Sometimes, a small act carried out with sincerity can mean so much to someone in need," he said.
He said Siti deserved recognition for the values she had displayed.
"She is proof that kindness, compassion and unity are still alive in our society," he said.
Lingeshwaran added that the incident reflected the Malaysia many people hoped to see, where individuals help one another regardless of race, religion or background.
He also congratulated Siti, describing her as an inspiration to fellow Malaysians.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man arrested after viral road rage incident in Bayan Lepas
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Police have arrested a 37-year-old man following a road rage incident where he allegedly criminally intimidated another driver and made obscene gestures along Jalan Tun Dr Awang on Thursday.
Southwest district police chief Assistant Commissioner Anuwal Ab Wahab said the incident occurred at 11.27am when the suspect, driving a Perodua Myvi, abruptly cut in front of the complainant's vehicle before stopping.
He said the suspect then stepped out of his car, wielding an object believed to be a metal rod and swung it towards the complainant's car,
"The incident was recorded by the complainant's dashboard camera, and the video later went viral on social media, drawing public attention," he said in a statement today.
Anuwal said police arrested the suspect following investigations into the incident.
"Checks revealed that the man has previous criminal and drug-related records, while a preliminary urine screening test found him positive for methamphetamine," he added.
Police also seized a Perodua Myvi, a PVC pipe, vehicle keys and clothing, believed to have been used during the incident to facilitate investigations.
The suspect was brought before the Balik Pulau magistrate's court and has been remanded for three days until May 31.
The case is being investigated under Section 506 of the Penal Code for criminal intimidation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Devotees flock to Penang temples ahead of Wesak as grand floral float nears completion
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Thousands of devotees have flocked to Buddhist temples across Penang ahead of Wesak Day tomorrow, as final touches are being made to the state's main procession float.
At the Malaysian Buddhist Association (MBA) here, where the towering 4.5m-tall float is being assembled, worshippers streamed in throughout the day while volunteers from the Penang Ohara Floral Art Society worked amid piles of freshly delivered flowers, transforming the structure into a vibrant sea of colours ahead of Sunday's state-level celebrations.
Devotees lit joss sticks and offered prayers inside the temple, while outside, teams of volunteers carefully arranged thousands of flowers that will adorn the float when it takes to the streets of George Town.
Floral arrangement adviser Chin Lee Siew, 80, said preparations began on Thursday and intensified after flowers started arriving two days ago.
The float, built on a white base, is now about 90 per cent complete.
"We received a very large number of flower donations this year. Some were worth thousands of ringgit. The response has been overwhelming," he told the New Straits Times.
As workers sorted blooms by colour and type, boxes of flowers continued arriving at the temple grounds, adding to an already impressive collection of chrysanthemums, orchids, carnations and other floral varieties.
The abundance of donations has been so significant that organisers have more flowers than they can use.
"There will be flowers left over after we complete the float. We will share them with other participating floats and donate the remainder to Buddhist temples.
"It shows that the spirit of giving remains strong. People are still contributing generously and supporting Wesak celebrations," Chin added.
The floral masterpiece will eventually be illuminated by LED lights, creating a striking visual spectacle when it joins 22 other floats in the annual Wesak procession.
For volunteer Teoh Yeam Chuan, 45, the preparations represented far more than decorative work.
Having visited the temple since childhood, he regarded the annual celebration as an enduring symbol of unity and goodwill.
"I've been coming here since I was young. Every year, the atmosphere is special because people gather not only to celebrate Wesak but also to pray for peace, happiness and harmony," he said.
Teoh said the procession has long served as a bridge between temples and the wider community.
"The floats are beautiful, but their purpose is deeper than that. They bring blessings to the people and inspire others to visit temples, reflect on life and do good deeds.
"It doesn't matter what race or religion you are. The message is universal — harmony, kindness and compassion for everyone," he said.
He said that before the age of social media and smartphones, the annual procession allowed people to experience Buddhist traditions and teachings firsthand.
"Today, it continues to serve as a source of inspiration. Someone watching the procession may feel encouraged to visit a temple, pray or simply become a better person. That's the spirit behind it," he added.
Among those visiting the temple ahead of Wesak Day was businessman Datuk Ang Thean Hock, 73, who has been attending prayers there for about 20 years.
"I come every year to pray for happiness, good health and prosperity.
"Wesak is a meaningful occasion because it reminds us to appreciate what we have and to do good for others," he said.
Preparations for the state-level celebration have been underway for the past two weeks.
This year's celebrations, themed "Bridging Through Digital, Cultivating Wisdom Together", will feature 23 floats from Buddhist temples and organisations across the state.
The oil lamp-lighting ceremony will take place this evening, while the grand procession will begin at 6pm tomorrow.
Wesak Day is the most sacred festival for Buddhists around the world as it commemorates three important events in the life of Siddhartha Gautama, who later became the Buddha: his birth as a prince in Lumbini, his attainment of enlightenment at the age of 35, and his passing into Nirvana at the age of 80.
The celebration takes place on the full moon of the ancient lunar month of Vesakha, which usually falls in May.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US warns capable of resuming war with Iran as deal remains elusive
Article:
WASHINGTON: The United States warned on Saturday it was "more than capable" of resuming war with Iran after President Donald Trump said any peace deal must adhere to his red lines, including Teheran never being able to develop nuclear weapons.
The White House had signalled Trump was close to a decision on an initial deal after weeks of mixed signals in tenuous negotiations, though Teheran denied there was a final agreement on ending the Middle East conflict that has jolted the global economy.
US sources had told AFP the deal was waiting on Trump's sign-off, but the president had made no decision after a two-hour meeting in the White House Situation Room on Friday in Washington.
Meanwhile, Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth, while attending a major Asia defence summit in Singapore, said on Saturday local time that Washington could restart the war if it wanted.
"Our ability to recommence if necessary is (that) we are more than capable, our stockpiles are more than suited for that, both there and around the globe because of how we balance exquisite and more plentiful munitions," he said.
That echoed the US Central Comand (CENTCOM), which posted on X that American forces "remain present and vigilant across the region."
The efforts to strike a deal mediated by Pakistan, were thrown into question this week by US strikes on the southern Iranian port of Bandar Abbas countered by retaliatory fire from Iran.
Nevertheless diplomacy continued including in a parallel process to stop fighting in Lebanon - which Iran has insisted be included in any formal end to the war and where Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu announced Israeli forces had advanced further even as military delegations from both nations met at the Pentagon in Washington.
Trump's priorities in any deal included Iran agreeing to never developing nuclear weapons and the re-opening of the blockaded Strait of Hormuz maritime route, he said in a social media post in which he also announced the Situation Room meeting.
"President Trump will only make a deal that is good for America and satisfies his red lines," a White House official told AFP after the meeting.
"Iran can never possess a nuclear weapon," the official added.
COMPETING CONDITIONS
Iranian foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei pushed back on Trump's conditions, telling state media that the Islamic republic "said goodbye to the language of 'must' 47 years ago."
Exchanges of messages were continuing, he added, but "no final agreement has been reached yet."
In a phone call with the Emir of Qatar, Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian said Iran was ready to achieve a "dignified framework" to end the war, according to state news agency IRNA.
In his post, Trump said Tehran would remove mines from the Strait of Hormuz and end its closure of the waterway with "no tolls," while the US would lift its parallel blockade of Iranian ports.
The two countries would also coordinate on removing and destroying Iran's enriched uranium, he said, adding that "no money will be exchanged, until further notice."
Iran's Fars news agency, however, cited sources as saying Tehran was demanding "the immediate release of US$12 billion in frozen Iranian assets" before moving to the next phase of negotiations.
On the toll-free reopening of Hormuz, the sources said "no such clause appears in the text of the agreement," while Trump's comment on destroying Iran's nuclear material "is fundamentally baseless."
Ali, a resident of the city of Tonekabon north of Tehran, said there would likely be more strife to come.
"Both sides are speaking in a way that keeps their supporters satisfied. It's not clear who is telling the truth," the 49-year-old said.
FIGHTING IN LEBANON
On the war's other main front in Lebanon, heavy fighting continued.
Netanyahu announced Friday that Israeli forces had advanced beyond a river that runs around 30 kilometres (20 miles) north of the Lebanon-Israel frontier.
Hizbollah also said its forces launched attacks on northern Israel as well as Israeli troops trying to advance near the medieval Beaufort fortress, also known as Qalaat al-Chakif, a site Israel's forces had used as a base during their two-decade occupation of southern Lebanon ending in 2000.
A ceasefire between Israel and Hizbollah was supposed to have taken effect on April 17, but has never been observed with both sides accusing each other of violating the truce.
Lebanon was drawn into the war in early March when Teheran-backed Hizbollah launched rockets at Israel over the killing of Iran's supreme leader in US-Israeli attacks, prompting Israeli strikes and a ground invasion.
Israel and Lebanon began direct talks in April, with a fourth round expected next week in Washington following the meeting of military delegations from both sides on Friday. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Derailed train cars cleared as Rapid KL works to restore normal services
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Four train cars that derailed recently near the Chan Sow Lin station have been successfully rerailed and returned to the depot for technical inspection.
Rapid KL, in a Facebook post today, said recovery works were carried out yesterday and early this morning, involving the coupling of the train cars with a specialised track vehicle to enable their safe removal.
"Efforts are being intensified to ensure safe and comfortable train services for passengers can be restored as soon as possible," the post said.
On Thursday, a train on the LRT Ampang/Sri Petaling Line
derailed
near the station following a fault in a track switch, disrupting service schedules.
Transport Minister Anthony Loke yesterday said the affected track was expected to resume operations no later than next Wednesday.
He said all stations along the Ampang/Sri Petaling Line
remained operational
, although services were running on a modified schedule to ensure train movements could continue throughout the recovery period.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police verify Perlis MB's firearm licence, seize shotgun
Article:
KANGAR: Police have confirmed that Perlis Menteri Besar Abu Bakar Hamzah holds a valid licence for the shotgun used to shoot a rampaging cow on Thursday.
District police chief Assistant Commissioner Yusharifuddin Mohd Yusop said checks by the district police headquarters' licensing division confirmed it.
He added that police seized the shotgun and nine rounds of buckshot to assist in investigations.
"Police received a report from the public at 4.51pm yesterday regarding a viral 38-second video of the incident at Dataran Lok 9, Kuala Perlis.
"A police team from the Kangar district headquarters recorded a statement from the menteri besar.
"Based on his statement, the action to discharge the firearm was taken because the cow was rampaging and posing an immediate threat to workers and the public at the Kuala Perlis Premier Korban Programme," he said in a statement today.
Yusharifuddin added that the case is being investigated under Section 39 of the Arms Act 1960 for discharging a firearm in a public place.
The offence carries a maximum penalty of one year in jail, a fine not exceeding RM2,000, or both, upon conviction.
Those with information can contact investigating officer Assistant Superintendent Tengku Mohd Farid Tengku Ibrahim at 019-9939200 or 04-9082222, or the nearest police station.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sustained, intelligence-led crackdown needed to dismantle underground vape market
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Enforcement authorities must adopt sustained, intelligence-led operations to dismantle the underground market for drug-laced vape products and halt vaping among schoolchildren.
Alliance for a Safe Community chairman Tan Sri Lee Lam Thye said sporadic enforcement alone is insufficient to tackle the black market trade, which spans covert physical networks and online sales.
"This underground vape market cannot be addressed through sporadic raids alone.
"It requires sustained enforcement, stronger intelligence gathering, stricter penalties, tighter control of online sales, and close cooperation among various enforcement agencies," he said.
Lee said while some illicit supply chains operate off the radar, others were still accessible through vape outlets, where a wide variety of products are openly sold.
"Underground operators are particularly difficult to detect as they are not visible and often operate through online channels, making enforcement more complex.
"This is more difficult to handle because they are not visible, they cannot be seen, and they can do a lot of online sales underground," he said.
Lee said intelligence gathering was the most important element in addressing the issue, as it would allow authorities to identify hotspots and networks before action is taken.
"This is where authorities have to focus on intelligence gathering. With intelligence gathering, then they will be able to more or less identify which are the spots, and then follow up with action," he said.
He also called for enforcement efforts to be strengthened around schools, as well as the provision of convenient reporting channels for vape-related activities.
"Enforcement around schools is needed because, around schools, sometimes there are shops selling vapes, and also provide access to the public to report illegal vape activities," he said.
Lee added that the situation had reached alarming levels, with nearly 20,000 student-related vaping cases recorded nationwide in 2024.
He warned that vaping had evolved from a nicotine issue into a broader public health threat, particularly with the emergence of drug-laced vape products.
"Vaping is no longer merely a nicotine-related issue. It is increasingly becoming a gateway to substance abuse among young Malaysians," he said.
Lee said the popularity of vaping among students was driven by attractive packaging, appealing flavours, aggressive online marketing, peer influence, easy accessibility and the misconception that vaping was harmless compared to conventional smoking.
"Many young people were unaware that nicotine addiction could develop rapidly and have long-term effects on brain development, concentration, mental health and academic performance.
"Drug-laced vape products posed an even greater risk, as they were harder for parents and teachers to detect and could lead to addiction, behavioural problems, impaired judgment and serious health complications.
As such, Lee said the issue must be curbed urgently for several reasons, including protecting the health and well-being of young people, preventing early addiction, safeguarding the school environment, stopping students from being drawn into drug distribution networks and avoiding a future public health burden.
He also warned that some students could be exploited by syndicates involved in illicit vape distribution.
Lee said among the measures he proposed were intensified enforcement against sales and promotion to minors, regular raids on physical premises and online platforms, heavier penalties on suppliers, and stronger inter-agency cooperation involving schools, parents, police, health authorities, customs and local authorities.
He also called for awareness and prevention programmes in schools, training for teachers, counsellors and parents to detect early signs of vaping and substance use, enhanced monitoring of online sales channels and stricter regulation of vape flavours, packaging, advertising and product displays that appeal to minors.
Lee said the fight against vaping among students could not be left to enforcement agencies alone and required a whole-of-society approach involving families, communities, healthcare professionals, educational institutions and government agencies.
"Our children are among the nation's most valuable assets. Allowing vaping, particularly drug-laced vaping, to spread unchecked risks creating a generation burdened by addiction, health problems and social challenges," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Anwar: Sabah interim special grant increased to RM1.5 billion
Article:
PENAMPANG: The interim special grant for Sabah has been increased to RM1.5 billion this year from RM600 million previously, said Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim.
He said the increase is a temporary measure pending a final mechanism for Sabah's 40 per cent revenue entitlement under the Malaysia Agreement 1963 (MA63).
"The actual figure for the 40 per cent revenue entitlement still needs to be finalised through negotiations between the federal and state governments," he added.
"On the 40 per cent revenue issue, although it is said to fall under financial arrangement 112D, in my view, it is settled. Do not dispute our decision to fulfil this promise," he said.
"Whether it is implemented in June or December must be decided by both committees and then brought to the state government at the highest level, followed by negotiations between the federal and state governments," he added.
He said this when officiating at the 2026 Sabah State-level Kaamatan Festival at Hongkod Koisaan, Kadazandusun Cultural Association (KDCA) here today.
Also present were Chief Minister Datuk Seri Hajiji Noor, Deputy Chief Minister I Datuk Seri Dr Joachim Gunsalam, who is also chairman of the 2026 Sabah State-level Kaamatan Festival Main Organising Committee, and KDCA president Tan Sri Joseph Pairin Kitingan.
Anwar said the commitment regarding the 40 per cent revenue payment has been brought to the Cabinet and officially announced in Parliament, but the current challenge lies mainly in its implementation mechanism.
The prime minister said he has tasked Second Finance Minister Datuk Seri Amir Hamzah Azizan and Treasury secretary-general Datuk Johan Mahmood Merican to refine negotiations with the Sabah state government.
He added that the negotiations must be carried out carefully, as any decision involving MA63 has direct implications for Sabah, Sarawak, and the states in Peninsular Malaysia.
"This decision is linked to the 1963 agreement (MA63). Therefore, every decision and its figures must be examined in detail. It takes some time, but it does not mean that the funds will not be provided," he said.
Countering claims that Sabah continues to be neglected, Anwar said the federal government collects around RM10 billion annually in revenue from Sabah, while development expenditure channelled back to the state amounts to a higher figure of RM17 billion.
The prime minister said the federal government also expects subsidies for petrol, diesel and liquefied petroleum gas in Sabah to rise to RM3 billion this year, compared with RM1 billion last year, following the ongoing global supply crisis.
He stressed that the disclosure of these figures is not intended to deny Sabah's 40 per cent revenue entitlement, but rather to provide a clearer picture to the public so they are not influenced by irresponsible political sentiment.
"Even amid soaring costs, the Madani Government remains committed to maintaining fuel prices, particularly RON95 petrol, at the lowest levels in the world to safeguard the welfare and livelihoods of the people.
"Do not deny (the federal government's efforts) and give the impression that we are being mistreated, treated as a stepchild, or made to suffer. This is petty politics. It will not foster unity. It is politics of misinformation, merely to seek support from certain ethnic groups, not based on facts," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Missing man sparks SAR operation, found asleep by washing machine next door
Article:
PADAWAN: A man who triggered a search and rescue operation after disappearing from a wedding reception in Kampung Sira here early today was found asleep next to a neighbour's washing machine.
A Sarawak Fire and Rescue Department operations centre spokesman said a distress call was received at 4.39am, prompting the deployment of a team from the Siburan fire station.
Two personnel in a Toyota Hilux pickup truck arrived at the scene at 5.14am to initiate the search alongside police officers, family members, and local villagers.
"Initial investigations revealed that the man, who is the brother-in-law of the complainant, had attended a wedding reception at a church in the village last night.
"After eating and drinking at the reception, he reportedly went outside to relieve himself but failed to return," he said.
Following a coordinated search effort, villagers discovered the man unhurt at 6am sleeping beside the household appliance next door.
The spokesman added that the operation was officially called off at 6.09am after medical checks confirmed the man sustained no injuries during his misadventure.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Civil society groups seek stronger checks in AG-PP separation bill
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Civil society groups have called on the government to ensure that proposed constitutional amendments separating the roles of the Attorney-General (AG) and Public Prosecutor (PP) result in an independent, accountable and credible prosecution service.
In a joint statement today, the groups urged the cabinet and members of parliament to closely scrutinise the proposed amendment and any recommendations made by the Parliamentary Special Select Committee (PSSC) before the legislation is debated and voted on in the Dewan Rakyat later this month.
The groups — Coalition for Clean and Fair Elections, Institute for Democracy and Economic Affairs, Rasuah Busters, Projek Sama and the Centre to Combat Corruption and Cronyism — warned that shortcomings in the proposed amendments could undermine the intended reform and create unintended constitutional consequences.
They said that while the bill was referred to the PSSC in March for further deliberation, neither the committee's report nor its proposed amendments had been made public.
The groups added that MPs must be given adequate time to review the committee's findings before being asked to debate or vote on constitutional changes of such significance.
"The integrity of the parliamentary process demands that MPs have full access to and sufficient time to review the PSSC's report and the proposed amendments before being asked to debate and vote on them.
"It is untenable for Parliament to enact constitutional amendments of this significance without MPs having had a genuine and meaningful opportunity to study the relevant materials," the statement said.
They called on the PSSC to immediately provide its report and proposed amendments to all MPs.
Should procedural rules prevent early disclosure, the groups said debate on the bill should be deferred until lawmakers and the public have had sufficient time to study the documents.
The civil society organisations also argued that the proposed reforms must include meaningful safeguards to ensure accountability in the appointment of the Public Prosecutor.
They said an independent prosecution service could only be achieved if the appointment process was transparent, accountable and protected from undue influence.
Among the measures proposed were requiring His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, to appoint the Public Prosecutor based on the advice of an independent body such as the Judicial and Legal Service Commission (JLSC), granting Parliament a substantive role in the appointment process, and publicly disclosing the names of shortlisted candidates.
The groups cautioned that placing sole responsibility for appointing the Public Prosecutor on the King could expose the constitutional monarchy to criticism if an appointee later proved unsuitable or abused the powers of the office.
"To safeguard the institution of the constitutional monarchy and ensure genuine accountability, the appointment process must clearly define responsibility and oversight."
The groups also urged the government to commit to introducing an enabling law to operationalise the constitutional amendments, including provisions governing the appointment process, parliamentary oversight, prosecutorial functions and ethical guidelines.
They said constitutional amendments alone would be insufficient without detailed legislation establishing how the new framework would function in practice.
In addition, the groups called for the Public Prosecutor's annual report to be tabled and debated in Parliament as part of efforts to strengthen accountability and transparency.
They urged the cabinet, which is scheduled to meet on June 5, and MPs debating the bill during the upcoming Dewan Rakyat sitting, to treat the reform as a matter of lasting constitutional significance.
"A constitutional reform that is rushed and inadequately deliberated may bring about grave unintended consequences and risks being worse than no reform at all."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar congratulates Tabung Haji, Amal on Saudi recognition
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has congratulated Lembaga Tabung Haji (TH) for receiving prestigious recognition from the Saudi Arabian Ministry of Hajj and Umrah.
This, Anwar said, reflects Malaysia's excellence in the management and welfare of pilgrims.
"This success reflects the commitment, professionalism and dedication of all TH staff and hajj officials who always strive to provide the best service in managing Malaysian pilgrims," he said in a social media post today.
Meanwhile, Anwar also extended congratulations to Amal by Malaysia Airlines, which received the "Misk Al-Khitam" award in recognition of its excellence in managing arrival procedures for pilgrims during this year's Hajj season.
He said the recognition demonstrates the capability and efficiency of Malaysian institutions and companies at the international level.
"May this success serve as a catalyst to further strengthen service quality and continue elevating the country's name in serving the guests of Allah," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Family of five escapes unhurt as suspected 'tekong darat' crashes car into house
Article:
TUMPAT: A family of five, including a 79-year-old grandmother and her two grandchildren, escaped unhurt this morning when a car driven by a suspected human trafficking agent crashed into their home.
The incident occurred at Kampung Tok Malik near Wakaf Bharu at 7am.
House owner Aminah Seman said she was reciting the Quran in her bedroom when she heard a loud noise outside the house.
"My daughter and I looked through the window and saw several men chasing another man. I immediately woke my grandchildren and we went outside to see what had happened," she said at her home.
At the time, her two grandchildren, aged 22 and 25, were asleep in the living room while her daughter and son-in-law were in another room.
The mother of nine was shocked to find that a car had crashed into their parked car before hitting the side of the house.
The impact damaged the vehicle and cracked the house structure.
"I am very grateful that my daughter, son-in-law, grandchildren, and I were not hurt.
"It is usually my routine to go outside every morning for exercise, but today I decided to stay indoors and recite the Quran instead. If I had been outside at the time, the situation could have been much worse," she said.
Aminah added that she later saw police detaining the suspect who was driving the vehicle.
Her son, Che Abdul Muiz Ibrahim, 38, said he was in Tanah Merah when he was informed of the incident.
"I rushed back to Wakaf Bharu immediately. I am thankful that my mother, sister, and the children are safe despite the frightening incident," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kazakhstan offers to take Iran's uranium stockpile, IAEA chief tells FT
Article:
LONDON: Kazakhstan has offered to take Iran's uranium stockpile if the United States and Iran reach an accord on Teheran's contested nuclear programme, UN nuclear watchdog chief Rafael Grossi told the Financial Times on Friday.
The head of the International Atomic Energy Agency met with Kazakhstan's President Kassym-Jomart Tokayev in Astana this week.
The Financial Times said the Kazakh leader had expressed his country's "openness" to store the stockpile enriched to near weapons grade level.
The estimated 440 kilogrammes of uranium processed to 60 percent purity is at the centre of talks between the United States and Iran on extending the ceasefire in the war unleashed by US-Israel attacks.
US President Donald Trump has insisted that Iran must accept that it will not have a nuclear weapon and that the uranium is destroyed. Iran has insisted on its right to maintain a nuclear programme.
According to US news site Axios, a deal being discussed does not solve the uranium dispute, but would include an Iranian commitment not to build a nuclear bomb. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Former MACC chief Azam Baki to file defamation suit against businessman Albert Tei next week
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 30 — Former Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said he will file a defamation suit against businessman Albert Tei next week, following allegations that he threatened and attempted to prevent the disclosure of claims linked to the Sabah corruption scandal.
Azam told
Astro Awani
today that the legal action was intended to defend his reputation and that of the anti-graft agency, while ensuring the public was made aware of the facts surrounding the matter.
“The public needs to know the facts. That is why I am taking legal action immediately through my lawyers,” he said.
According to Azam, his lawyers are preparing the necessary documents and the suit is expected to be filed on Wednesday.
He also rejected Tei’s allegations, saying he had never spoken to, met or had any dealings with the businessman.
“I have never spoken to him. I have never met him. I do not know what is meant by the allegations that have been made,” he reportedly said.
Azam added that even his legal representatives had never dealt with Tei, describing the claims as an attempt to mislead the public and damage the reputations of both himself and the MACC.
The dispute follows a police report lodged by Tei at the Putrajaya district police headquarters, in which he alleged that Azam had threatened him and sought to stop him from exposing the Sabah corruption scandal in November 2024.
Tei claimed in the report that he possessed documentary evidence relating to the alleged incident, including a list of individuals who were aware of it.

Title: ‘A true patriot’: Dzulkefly hails IJN cardiothoracic surgeon Dr Jeswant Dillon’s historic AATS induction
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 30 — Prof Datuk Seri Dr Jeswant Dillon’s induction as the first Malaysian into the American Association for Thoracic Surgery (AATS) represents the highest form of recognition for his excellence, dedication and contributions to the field of cardiothoracic surgery.
Health Minister Datuk Seri Dzulkefly Ahmad said this achievement was not attained overnight but is the result of the surgeon’s ongoing commitment and years of devoted service in advancing medical care.
“He is a true patriot who has proudly raised the Jalur Gemilang on the international stage. I have personally witnessed his precision and expertise in the operating theatre.
“On behalf of the Ministry of Health and the government, I extend my deepest respect to Dr Jeswant for this truly well-deserved recognition. Malaysia is immensely proud of your contributions and achievements,” he said in a Facebook post today.
Dr Dzulkefly added that the success of the senior consultant cardiothoracic surgeon at the National Heart Institute (IJN) has not only brought honour to the nation but also serves as an inspiration to the next generation of Malaysian surgeons, encouraging them to pursue excellence and achieve even greater milestones. — Bernama

Title: PKR election director reshuffle is about strategy, not personalities, says Youth chief
Article:
PADANG RENGAS, May 30 — The change in PKR’s election director post is part of a strategic restructuring exercise aimed at strengthening the party machinery ahead of the next election.
Angkatan Muda Keadilan (AMK) chief Muhammad Kamil Abdul Munim said changes in portfolios, whether within a political party or the government, are a normal practice undertaken based on current requirements.
He said the restructuring of responsibilities within the party should not be construed as reflecting any weakness on the part of its leaders, including Nurul Izzah Anwar, who now serves as PKR deputy president.
“There is no question of her being unqualified or incapable because she has previously served as election director, including during the 14th General Election, when the party successfully formed the government.
“This is merely a strategic restructuring to ensure election-related operations can be carried out more effectively and efficiently,” he told reporters at the Padang Rengas Madani Youth Carnival here today.
On the appointment of PKR vice-president Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari as the party’s election director alongside Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail, Muhammad Kamil said both leaders possess extensive experience in managing election operations and organisation.
“Previously, Amirudin served as election operations director, giving him the knowledge and experience required. I am confident he will be able to carry out this responsibility effectively,” he said.
Meanwhile, on Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim’s remarks on the possibility of cost-saving measures in Budget 2027 amid global economic uncertainty, he described the move as a responsible approach.
“Any responsible leader would review the country’s economic position and align expenditure with current needs. Although such measures may be viewed as unpopular, they are important to ensure the long-term sustainability of the country’s economy,” he said.
He also stressed that any restructuring of government expenditure would not affect critical sectors such as healthcare and education.
“Related programmes will continue to be implemented, but the government will review its spending methods and approaches to identify areas where savings can be made,” he said. — Bernama

Title: Minister says RM1.5b Sabah interim grant shows federal government is listening
Article:
KOTA KINABALU, May 30 — The increase in the special interim grant for Sabah, from RM600 million to RM1.5 billion, demonstrates the federal government’s ongoing commitment to addressing the state’s rightful claims gradually and responsibly.
Minister in the Prime Minister’s Department (Sabah and Sarawak), Datuk Mustapha Sakmud, said the announcement by Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim marks a significant achievement, reflecting the Madani Government’s determination to ensure that Sabah’s interests continue to receive due attention.
"For the record, before the change of federal government in 2022, Sabah received only about RM26.7 million per year for decades, despite the 40 per cent revenue entitlement being enshrined in the Federal Constitution," he said in a statement today.
He noted that under the current prime minister’s administration, Sabah has seen substantial year-on-year increases: RM125.6 million in 2022; RM300 million in 2023 and 2024; RM600 million in 2025 and 2026; and now the latest hike, raising it to RM1.5 billion.
Mustapha emphasised that this consistent upward trend proves that the voices and demands of Sabah's people are not only being heard but are also being taken seriously by the Federal Government.
Mustapha said the issue of Sabah's 40 per cent revenue entitlement has dragged on for decades. Resolving it fully, he said, requires strong political commitment and close cooperation between the state and federal governments.
At the same time, he emphasised that the pursuit of Sabah's rights will continue until a more comprehensive and final settlement is reached, consistent with the spirit of the Malaysia Agreement 1963 (MA63) as well as Articles 112C and 112D of the Federal Constitution.
Therefore, he called for the continued application of a mature, prudent and forward-looking approach. This would ensure that Sabah's rights can be realised without undermining the stability and good relations within the Malaysian Federation.
Mustapha also expressed confidence that under Anwar's leadership and the Madani vision, empowering Sabah and Sarawak will remain a priority for the federal government. — Bernama

Title: Anwar says government cuts its own spending to channel more aid to rakyat
Article:
PENAMPANG, May 30 — Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said the Madani Government has implemented strict cost-cutting measures across ministries, with the savings fully redirected towards programmes and initiatives that benefit the people.
He said allocations for ministries would be reduced for non-essential spending, although critical sectors such as education, healthcare and basic infrastructure, including in Sabah, would be spared.
